section.title: Fight the Good Fight
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes with a stirring personal charge. Timothy, the man of God, is to flee the love of money and pursue godliness, fighting the good fight of the faith until the appearing of Christ, whose coming the blessed and only Sovereign will bring about. Paul adds instructions for the rich to be generous, and a final plea: guard the deposit entrusted to you.
section.thesis: The man of God flees greed and fights the good fight of faith, keeping Christ's command until His appearing, so that the rich hold wealth loosely and generously and the whole trust of the gospel is guarded to the end, to the glory of the blessed and only Sovereign.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the charge to fight the good fight, crowned with the doxology to the blessed and only Sovereign; the charge to the rich to be generous; and the final plea to guard the entrusted deposit.

verses.text: But you, O man of God, flee from these things and pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, perseverance, and gentleness.
{{agonizomai|Fight the good fight}} of the faith. Take hold of the eternal life to which you were called when you made the good {{homologia|confession}} before many witnesses.
I charge you in the presence of God, who gives life to all things, and of Christ Jesus, who made the good confession in His testimony before Pontius Pilate:
Keep this commandment without stain or reproach until the {{epiphaneia|appearance}} of our Lord Jesus Christ,
which the blessed and only {{dynastes|Sovereign One}}, the King of kings and Lord of lords, will bring about in His own time.
He alone is immortal and dwells in unapproachable light. No one has ever seen Him, nor can anyone see Him. To Him be honor and eternal dominion! Amen.
Instruct those who are rich in the present age not to be conceited and not to put their hope in the uncertainty of wealth, but in God, who richly provides all things for us to enjoy.
Instruct them to do good, to be rich in good works, and to be generous and ready to share,
treasuring up for themselves a firm foundation for the future, so that they may take hold of that which is truly life.

units.label: Guard the deposit
units.note: keep the trust to the end

verses.text: O Timothy, guard what has been {{parathede|entrusted}} to you. Avoid irreverent, empty chatter and the opposing arguments of so-called “{{gnosis|knowledge}},”
which some have professed and thus swerved away from the faith. Grace be with you all.

agonizomai.gloss: To contend, struggle, or compete, as in an athletic contest or battle; the root of our word agony. Paired with the noun for a good fight, it pictures the Christian life and ministry as a strenuous contest to be fought with all one's strength, taking hold of eternal life.

homologia.gloss: A confession, an open acknowledgment made with the mouth, literally a saying the same thing. Timothy made the good confession before witnesses, as Christ did before Pilate. Public confession of the faith is a solemn, binding act that shapes the whole of life.

epiphaneia.gloss: An appearing or manifestation, used of the visible coming of Christ; the root of our word epiphany. Timothy is to keep Christ's command spotless until this appearing, giving the whole charge an eye toward the certain return of the Lord in glory.

dynastes.gloss: A ruler, sovereign, or potentate, one who holds power; the root of our word dynasty. God is the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords, whose unrivaled majesty and power will bring about Christ's appearing in His own time.

parathede.gloss: A deposit, something entrusted to another for safekeeping. Timothy is to guard the deposit, the gospel and sound teaching handed to him, keeping it intact and passing it on unspoiled, the letter's final and summarizing charge.

gnosis.gloss: Knowledge. Paul warns against what is falsely called knowledge, the pretentious speculation of the false teachers. The phrase may point to an early form of the error later known as gnosticism; true knowledge is found in the gospel deposit, not in these empty oppositions.

basic.context: Paul makes his final, personal appeal. Timothy, the man of God, must flee the love of money and pursue righteousness and godliness, fighting the good fight of the faith and keeping Christ's command until He appears. This bursts into praise of God, the blessed and only Sovereign, immortal and dwelling in unapproachable light. Paul then tells the rich to be generous and to hope in God, and ends with the charge to guard the gospel entrusted to Timothy.
basic.hermeneutics: The charge is direct and prescriptive: flee greed, pursue godliness, fight for the faith, guard the deposit. The doxology teaches us who God is. The instructions to the rich are timeless wisdom for anyone with means, redirecting hope from wealth to God and calling for generosity.
basic.originalLanguages: Timothy is to agonizomai, fight the good fight, having made the good homologia, confession. He keeps the command until the epiphaneia, appearing, of Christ, which the blessed dynastes, Sovereign, will bring. His last task is to guard the parathede, deposit, against what is falsely called gnosis, knowledge.
basic.typology: The good confession before Pilate marks Christ as the faithful witness whose testimony Timothy's own confession echoes. The King of kings and Lord of lords, veiled here in unapproachable light, is fully revealed in Christ at His appearing.
basic.christ: Christ made the good confession before Pontius Pilate, standing as the faithful witness Timothy is to imitate. The whole charge looks to His epiphaneia, His glorious appearing, which the sovereign God will bring about in His own time. The gospel of Christ is the very deposit Timothy must guard to the end.
basic.god: God is the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords, alone immortal, dwelling in unapproachable light, unseen and unseeable, worthy of honor and eternal dominion. He gives life to all things and richly provides all we enjoy. He is the great reality that dwarfs the love of money and steadies the fight of faith.
basic.crossReferences: Second Timothy 4 records Paul's own finished fight and kept faith. Revelation 19 names Christ King of kings and Lord of lords. Matthew 6 tells us to store up treasure in heaven, echoing the charge to the rich.
basic.oneStory: The whole letter, and the whole Bible, aims at the day when the blessed and only Sovereign brings Christ's appearing. Timothy is charged to fight and to guard the gospel until then. His faithfulness belongs to God's long work of keeping His truth alive across the generations until the King of kings is revealed in glory.

context.literary: The close gathers the letter's threads: fleeing the love of money just condemned, pursuing the godliness urged throughout, and guarding the sound doctrine at stake from chapter 1. The soaring doxology in verses 15 and 16 is a high point of New Testament praise, and the letter ends abruptly with the charge to guard the deposit and a brief benediction.
context.historical: The good confession may recall Timothy's baptism or ordination before witnesses. The reference to Christ before Pilate roots the confession in history. The warning against what is falsely called knowledge fits an emerging speculative movement in Ephesus, an early strand of what would become gnosticism.
context.canonical: The doxology stands with the great confessions of God's majesty in Scripture, and the charge to guard the deposit is echoed in 2 Timothy, framing the Pastoral Epistles as a handing-on of the faith. The title King of kings and Lord of lords reappears for Christ in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To charge Timothy personally to flee greed, pursue godliness, fight the good fight, and guard the gospel deposit until Christ's appearing, and to direct the rich toward hope in God and generosity, all under the majesty of the sovereign God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The charges to Timothy and to the rich are directly prescriptive for believers in every age. The doxology describes God's nature for our worship. The good confession before witnesses describes Timothy's experience while modeling the public confession every believer is called to make and keep.

debates.question: What was the good confession Timothy made before many witnesses?

views.label: His baptismal confession
views.case: Many link it to Timothy's profession of faith at baptism, publicly owning Christ before the gathered church.

views.label: His ordination or a moment of testimony under pressure
views.case: Others tie it to his commissioning to ministry, or to a bold confession made before hostile witnesses, paralleling Christ before Pilate in the next verse.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: a public, binding confession of Christ that Timothy, like his Lord, is now to live up to without wavering.

debates.question: What is the so-called knowledge in verse 20?

views.label: The Ephesian false teachers' speculation
views.case: It names the myths, genealogies, and empty controversies condemned throughout the letter, a pretentious knowledge that puffs up but does not build.

views.label: An early form of gnosticism
views.case: The Greek word gnosis and the later rise of gnostic sects lead some to see an incipient version of that error here, though in seed form.

debates.bearing: Both agree that this knowledge is a counterfeit set against the gospel deposit; the remedy is not more speculation but guarding the entrusted truth.

study.originalLanguages: Timothy, the man of God, is to flee greed and agonizomai, contend or fight, the good fight of faith, a word carrying the strain of an athletic contest, the root of agony. He has made the good homologia, confession, the same word used of Christ's testimony before Pilate. He must keep the command spotless until the epiphaneia, the appearing, of Christ, which the blessed dynastes, Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords, will bring about in His own time. The letter ends on its keynote: guard the parathede, the deposit entrusted to you, against the empty oppositions of what is falsely named gnosis, knowledge.

typology.fulfillment: Christ the faithful witness, Revelation 1:5, whose bold testimony Timothy's own confession echoes and is to imitate.

typology.type: The King of kings dwelling in unapproachable light
typology.fulfillment: The unseen God fully revealed in Christ, whose glorious appearing the Sovereign will bring in His own time.

study.christ: Christ made the good confession before Pontius Pilate, standing as the faithful and true witness whose steadfastness Timothy is to imitate in his own confession. The entire charge is oriented to Christ's epiphaneia, His glorious appearing, which the sovereign God will bring about at the appointed time. And the deposit Timothy must guard to the end is nothing other than the gospel of this Christ, the sound words of our Lord Jesus that the whole letter has defended.
study.god: The passage soars into worship of God as the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords, alone possessing immortality, dwelling in unapproachable light, unseen and beyond sight, to whom belong honor and eternal dominion. He gives life to all things and richly provides everything for us to enjoy. This towering vision of God is what steadies the fight of faith and exposes the smallness of wealth in which the rich are tempted to trust.

crossReferences.note: I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, the charge here lived out to the end by Paul.
King of kings and Lord of lords, the title given here to God and there to the returning Christ.
Store up treasure in heaven, echoing the rich treasuring up a firm foundation for the future.
Guard the good deposit through the Holy Spirit, the same charge to keep the entrusted gospel intact.

study.oneStory: The whole letter, and the whole Bible, presses toward the day when the blessed and only Sovereign brings about the appearing of Christ. Timothy is charged to fight the good fight and to guard the gospel deposit until then, holding wealth loosely and hope firmly in God. His faithfulness belongs to God's long work of keeping His truth alive across the generations until the King of kings is revealed in unveiled glory.

quiz.q: What is Timothy, as a man of God, told to flee and to pursue?

options.text: Flee the love of money; pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, perseverance, and gentleness.
options.note: Verse 11 gives both the flight and the pursuit.

options.text: Flee all hardship; pursue a comfortable life.
options.note: He is called to fight, not to seek comfort; the pursuit is godliness, not ease.

options.text: Flee other believers; pursue solitude.
options.note: The flight is from greed and its evils, not from the church.

options.text: Flee doctrine; pursue personal freedom.
options.note: He is to guard doctrine, not flee it.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by fight the good fight of the faith?

options.text: To contend earnestly for the faith and take hold of eternal life.
options.note: Verse 12 casts the Christian life as a strenuous contest with an eternal prize.

options.text: To start quarrels with everyone who disagrees.
options.note: It is a fight for the faith and toward godliness, not mere quarreling.

options.text: To win political power for the church.
options.note: The fight is for faith and eternal life, not earthly power.

options.text: To avoid all struggle and coast along.
options.note: The image is a demanding contest, the opposite of coasting.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe God in the doxology of verses 15 and 16?

options.text: The blessed and only Sovereign, King of kings and Lord of lords, alone immortal, dwelling in unapproachable light.
options.note: Verses 15 and 16 give this majestic praise.

options.text: One ruler among many rival powers.
options.note: He is the only Sovereign, the King of kings, not one among rivals.

options.text: A being fully visible to all who look.
options.note: Paul says no one has seen Him nor can see Him.

options.text: A distant deity with no involvement in the world.
options.note: He gives life to all things and will bring about Christ's appearing.

quiz.q: What is Paul's charge to those who are rich?

options.text: Not to trust in wealth but in God, and to be generous and rich in good works.
options.note: Verses 17 to 19 redirect their hope and call for generosity.

options.text: To feel guilty and give away everything at once.
options.note: He calls for generosity and right hope, not guilt-driven abandonment; God gives all things to enjoy.

options.text: To enjoy wealth with no thought for others.
options.note: They are to be generous and ready to share, not self-indulgent.

options.text: To put their full confidence in their riches.
options.note: Their hope is to be in God, not in uncertain wealth.

quiz.q: What is Paul's final charge to Timothy?

options.text: Guard what has been entrusted to you, avoiding empty chatter and false knowledge.
options.note: Verse 20 gives the closing charge to guard the deposit.

options.text: Pursue the latest speculative knowledge.
options.note: He is to avoid what is falsely called knowledge, not chase it.

options.text: Keep the gospel to himself.
options.note: He guards it to keep it intact and pass it on, not to hoard it.

options.text: Abandon the faith if it becomes difficult.
options.note: He is to guard the faith to the end, warning against those who swerved from it.

questions: Paul calls Timothy to both flee and pursue. What specifically do you most need to flee right now, and what should you be running toward instead?
The Christian life is a good fight, not a stroll. Where are you fighting well for the faith, and where have you gone passive?
The doxology lifts our eyes to the blessed and only Sovereign. How does a bigger vision of God shrink the things that tempt and frighten you?
The rich are told to hope in God, not wealth, and to be generous. Whatever your means, where is your hope quietly resting, and how generous are you?
Timothy was to guard the deposit of the gospel. What has been entrusted to you to keep and pass on faithfully, and how are you guarding it?

translationNotes.note: Where the BSB prints em dashes around the King of kings and Lord of lords, house style renders them as commas: the blessed and only Sovereign One, the King of kings and Lord of lords, will bring about.
Fight the good fight renders a noun and verb from the same root, agon, picturing a strenuous athletic or military contest, echoed in 2 Timothy 4:7.
So-called knowledge translates a phrase, falsely named knowledge, gnosis; many see here an early form of the error later called gnosticism.
The BSB footnote notes that some manuscripts add Amen at the very end of the letter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 6:11-21 this session. Em dashes in verse 15 have been rendered as commas per house style.
